behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: the failure is at the handoff between signature verification and queue forwarding (handler must both return 401 and avoid enqueue). Rejected higher levels: no behavior text requires real OS/network/subprocess; this can be caught with in-process handler + queue spy/stub. No AI output dependency, no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Wire HTTP handler, real signature-check module, and in-process queue adapter spy. Send a request with mismatched raw-body/HMAC header. Assert 401 and assert enqueue was not called.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test requires module interaction (handler + verifier + queue), so Integration is necessary and sufficient.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: this is an ordering guarantee across handler/queue interface (persist then ack). Rejected System: behavior text does not require real network or OS primitives; ordering can be proven with in-process wiring and controllable queue implementation. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Use handler + concrete persistent-queue module (or test double that records durable-write completion) in-process. Delay queue write completion; verify 200 is not emitted until write succeeds, and is emitted immediately after.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The assertion is cross-module sequencing; Integration captures it without real external infra.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: failure appears at handoff between enqueue path and async downstream worker lifecycle. Rejected System: no requirement for real subprocess/network; in-process delayed downstream worker reproduces wait-vs-no-wait bug. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with queue + downstream consumer stub that blocks for a long promise. Send valid event; assert HTTP response completes before consumer promise resolves.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Needs multiple modules (handler, queue, consumer) but no real OS/network.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Integration -> System proof: this is a real-time SLA under concurrency; event-loop scheduling, socket handling, and wall-clock latency under concurrent load are OS/runtime effects that mocks can hide. Rejected Agentic: no model-output quality/non-determinism in behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real Node HTTP server, generate concurrent requests while earlier events are processed slowly, and measure end-to-end client-observed latency. Assert each new webhook response is <3s wall-clock.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A lower-level in-process fake clock test could miss real runtime contention; System is required.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: dedup correctness depends on handler + idempotency store/queue interaction across requests. Rejected System: behavior text does not require real network/OS behavior; in-process repeated calls can expose duplicate-write bug. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler with real dedup logic and queue adapter in-process. Send two requests with same event ID. Assert first is enqueued, second is acknowledged as duplicate (per API contract) and not enqueued again.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Cross-module state handoff is essential; Integration is the minimum proven boundary.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: failure is at interface translation from queue-full error/state to HTTP status+header. Rejected System: no concrete need for real OS/network/subprocess; an in-process queue implementation that returns full is enough. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with queue module configured to return/full error on enqueue. Send valid request; assert HTTP 503 and presence/value format of Retry-After, and no downstream processing start.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The behavior is adapter logic across modules, not infrastructure-dependent.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: this validates queue implementation against its persistence/config boundary (capacity accounting + reject behavior), which is beyond a single pure function. Rejected System: behavior text does not explicitly require external queue service, real network, or subprocess; can be validated with concrete queue module on local storage. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue implementation with small capacity in a test environment (e.g., temp local store). Fill to limit, then push one more event and assert deterministic rejection and unchanged stored count.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test uses real queue module + storage wiring, so Integration is needed; System only if queue is truly external.
